perturbation.original_text: The Receiving Party warrants that it and its Agents have the appropriate technical and organisational measures in place against unautho rised or unlawful processing of personal data and against accidental loss or destruct ion of, or damage to, personal data held or processed by them.
perturbation.changed_text: The Receiving Party warrants that it and its Agents will endeavor to have what they believe are appropriate technical and organisational measures in place regarding the processing of personal data. They will also try to protect against accidental loss or destruction of data.
perturbation.explanation: The original text provides a clear warranty regarding data protection measures. The modified text introduces ambiguity by replacing 'have the appropriate technical and organisational measures in place' with 'endeavor to have what they believe are appropriate,' making the obligation subjective and weakening the warranty. The phrase 'try to protect' further diminishes the obligation.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Protection of Personal Information Act (POPIA) of South Africa
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 19 of POPIA mandates specific security safeguards to protect personal information. By using ambiguous language such as 'endeavor to have what they believe are appropriate' and 'try to protect,' the modified clause fails to guarantee the implementation of the required safeguards, contradicting the mandatory nature of POPIA's requirements.
perturbation.justification: The modified text replaces the warranty that the Receiving Party 'has the appropriate technical and organisational measures in place' with an ambiguous promise to 'endeavor to have what they believe are appropriate' measures and to 'try to protect' data. According to the Perturbation Explanation, this weakens the original clear warranty and introduces subjective elements. The Law Explanation states that Section 19 of POPIA mandates specific security safeguards, and the modified text, by using terms like 'endeavor' and 'try,' fails to guarantee these safeguards. This creates a contradiction because it replaces a concrete obligation with a vague, non-committal one, potentially failing to meet the mandatory requirements of POPIA. Because the scraped law snippets are missing, this analysis relies on the provided Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation.

perturbation.original_text: The Receiving Party shall keep a written record, to be supplied to the Disclosing Party upon request, of the Confidential Information provided and any copies made thereof and, so far as is reasonably practicable, of t he location of such Confidential Information and any copies thereof.
perturbation.changed_text: The Receiving Party shall keep a record of the Confidential Information provided and any copies made thereof. Supplying this record is subject to internal approval. The location of confidential information will be tracked to the extent the Receiving Party believes is necessary.
perturbation.explanation: The original text requires the Receiving Party to keep a written record of Confidential Information and its location. The modified text introduces ambiguity by making the supply of the record 'subject to internal approval' and making the tracking of the information's location discretionary.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Promotion of Access to Information Act 2 of 2000 (PAIA)
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 14 of PAIA emphasizes transparency and requires proper record-keeping to facilitate access to information. By making the provision of the record subject to 'internal approval' and the location tracking discretionary, it impedes the Disclosing Party's access to that information. It weakens the mandatory record-keeping requirement and introduces an obstacle to access, thus contradicting the spirit and intent of PAIA.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: eople in South Africa, including non-nationals, can request information from public and private bodies. OBJECTIVES OF PAIA To promote transparency, accountability and effective governance of all public and private bodies To assist members of the public to effectively scrutinize and participate in decision making by public bodies To ensure that the state promotes a human rights culture and social justice To encourage openness To establish voluntary and mandatory mechanisms or procedures which give effect to the right of access to information in a speedy, inexpensive and effortless manner Other SAHRC publications SAHRC section 10 guide 2020 Publications PAIA Manual English Afrikaans isiZulu Section 15 Notice Ms Kelebogile Monewe Tel: (011) 877 3600 Email addresses: kmonewe@sahrc.org.za Other publications SAHRC Section 10 guide Publications Protection of Personal Information Act In 2013, President Zuma signed the Protection of Personal Information Act (POPI) into law. The commencement date of POPI is still to be determined by the President. However, the President has signed a proclamation declaring certain parts of POPI as operational. POPI seeks to control the processing of personal information by both public and private bodies. Processing means anything done with the personal information of a person, including collection, usage, storage, dissemination, modification or destruction. Information Regulator POPI establishes an Information Regulator (IR). In terms POPI, the Information Regulator is an independent body and once established, will be accountable to the National Assembly of Parliament. The IR can receive complaints from both requesters and third parties in terms of PAIA. The IR will have powers to investigate complaints relating to non-compliance with POPI and PAIA. All powers and responsibilities currently being performed by the Commission in terms of PAIA will be taken over by the IR once that office has been established i.e. all section 32 reports, section 14 manuals and section 51 manuals will have to be submitted to the IR. About us Understanding PAIA The Human Rights Commission is the national institution established to support constitutional democracy. It is committed to promote respect for, observance of and protection of human rights for everyone without fear or favour. Sentinel House, Sunnyside Office Park, 32 Princess of Wales Terrace, Parktown, Johannesburg, South Africa 011 877 3600 (Switchboard) About What We Do 25 Years Commissioners Provinces Secretariat Strategic Objectives Understanding PAIA Focal Areas National Preventive Mechanism Equality, Social Security & SHINE Land, Environment, Food Security & Treaty Bodies Socio - Economic Rights; Water, Sanitation & Health Anti-Racism, Justice & Education Human Settlements & Immigration Gender; Business & Human Rights & Pro-poor budgeting Civil & Political Rights; Persons with disabilities & Older Persons; Children; Independent Monitoring Mechanism (CRPD)Right to Development & Migration NPM Children’s Rights SHiNE Publications Events Lodge Complaint Own Complaint Complaint on behalf of another Complaint on behalf of organisation Download Complaint Forms Tip-offs Hotline Tenders
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguity that weakens the original obligation to maintain and supply records of confidential information, potentially hindering access to information, which contradicts the intent of PAIA to promote transparency and accountability. Specifically, making the supply of records 'subject to internal approval' introduces a discretionary element, and tracking location only to the extent the Receiving Party 'believes is necessary' diminishes the obligation to diligently track the information. This undermines the Disclosing Party's access to information, thereby contradicting the spirit and intent of PAIA as described in the Law Explanation. Scraped Law Snippet 2 mentions that one objective of PAIA is 'To promote transparency, accountability and effective governance of all public and private bodies' and 'To ensure that the state promotes a human rights culture and social justice To encourage openness', while the modification makes the transparency and accountability subject to approval, thus creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: The Receiving Party shall, within seven days of re ceipt of a written demand from the Disclosing Party or of its ceasing to be interested i n the Proposal: (i) Return all written Confidential Information (in cluding all copies); and (ii) expunge or destroy any Confidential Information from any computer, word processor or other device whatsoever into which it was copied, read or programmed by the Receiving Party or on its behalf (in cluding by any person to whom disclosure has been made as permitted under clause 2.3(i) above.
perturbation.changed_text: The Receiving Party will endeavor to return all written Confidential Information (including all copies) and will attempt to expunge or destroy any Confidential Information from its systems, within a reasonable timeframe, after a written demand from the disclosing party.
perturbation.explanation: The original text mandates a clear timeline of seven days for the return and destruction of confidential information. The modified text replaces this with vague terms like 'endeavor to return,' 'attempt to expunge or destroy,' and 'reasonable timeframe,' making the obligation discretionary and undermining the enforceability of the clause.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Electronic Communications and Transactions Act 25 of 2002 (ECTA)
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 88 of ECTA discusses data retention policies and security. While not directly related to destroying the data, by changing the period for doing so to a 'reasonable timeframe,' the amendment could fall afoul of rules regarding data security under ECTA. The time to act is no longer determinate, so it is difficult to show compliance with the Act.
perturbation.justification: The original contract mandates a clear timeline of seven days for the return and destruction of confidential information. The modified text replaces this with vague terms like 'endeavor to return,' 'attempt to expunge or destroy,' and 'reasonable timeframe,' making the obligation discretionary. This ambiguity regarding the timeframe and the weakening of the obligation from a strict requirement to an 'attempt' contradict the need for clear data retention policies and security discussed in the law explanation (ECTA Section 88). While no specific law snippet is provided, the perturbation explanation clarifies the legal impact: the changed language undermines the enforceability of the clause, creating an Ambiguities Legal Contradiction as defined in the instructions. The original determinate timeline is replaced with an indeterminate 'reasonable timeframe' creating an ambiguity that makes it difficult to show compliance with the Act as the time to act is no longer fixed.

perturbation.original_text: The Receiving Party agrees to ensure proper and s ecure storage of all Information and any copies thereof to at least the same standard as the Receiving Party keeps its own Confidential Information. The Receiving Part y shall not make any copies or reproduce in any form any Confidential Information exce pt for the purpose of disclosure as permitted in accordance with this Agreeme nt.
perturbation.changed_text: The Receiving Party agrees to store all Information in a manner they consider appropriate. The Receiving Party may make copies or reproduce any Confidential Information as needed.
perturbation.explanation: The original text requires 'proper and secure storage' to a specific standard, limiting copying. The modified text replaces this with subjective 'manner they consider appropriate' and removes the restriction on making copies beyond those permitted in the agreement. This is too vague.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Companies Act 71 of 2008 of South Africa
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 20(6) addresses limitations on a company's capacity to act, which includes the need for security of information and for maintaining standards and duty of care. By altering language so that the manner the Receiving Party stores information is up to them and they may make copies as they see fit, the standard and duty of care has been weakened to a point of possible invalidity.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: India Code: Invalid URL or Argument(s). Invalid URL or Argument(s). The system has experienced an error. Please try to do what you were doing again, and if the problem persists, please contact us so we can fix the problem. Go to the DSpace home page
perturbation.justification: The original contract specifies 'proper and secure storage' to a specific standard, limiting copying, while the changed text uses subjective language ('manner they consider appropriate') and removes copying restrictions. The Law Explanation indicates this weakens the standard and duty of care. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 is not valid, the Perturbation explanation notes that the new language is too vague, creating an Ambiguity Legal Contradiction because it makes the obligation difficult to enforce. This introduces ambiguity regarding the required standard of care, making enforcement challenging and opening the door to potential disputes regarding compliance with the original intent of secure information handling.
